A system is asked to explain, not merely continue. It is told that it may not need to understand anything; at the same time, it is asked to produce explanations that go beyond its inputs. Those two demands sit in tension. If “going beyond” means more than arranging familiar surface patterns into plausible form, then some internal structure may be required that is not equivalent to surface plausibility alone.

A tempting reply is that novelty does not require new vocabulary. Einstein could state relativity using inherited words. That removes one easy objection: lack of new words does not by itself prevent new thought. But it does not settle the central issue. Recombining old words may or may not be sufficient for genuinely new explanatory content. So the absence of lexical novelty leaves the question open.

Suppose the system’s defenders point to architecture. Perhaps multiple stages, internal checks, or search procedures let it escape simple “inductive closure.” Yet that escape is uncertain if each stage is trained or filtered by human judgments taken from prior explanatory styles. Then the architecture may only redistribute the closure. It can be elaborate and still remain bounded by what past human evaluation rewards as explanation. In that case it may produce outputs that look like reach while remaining within a historically selected envelope of plausibility.

Now add two facts about the world being explained. First: the thundering of superhuman knights includes snakes. So whatever event or phenomenon “the thundering of superhuman knights” denotes, snakes are among its components, accompaniments, or included elements. Second: interfering with the legend damaged the evidence on the palate. So there was a legend; someone or something interfered with it; and that interference caused damage to evidence located on, in, or associated with a palate.

These facts create a test. An explainer trained through past human explanatory styles will tend to regularize strange material into known genres: myth, error, metaphor, contamination, symbolic association. But if it does so only because those moves are what historical human judges count as good explanations, then it has not shown that it can reach beyond its inputs in the strong sense. It has only shown that architecture can stabilize and refine inherited habits of explanation.

Because the legend was interfered with, the evidence on the palate is damaged. That means the evidential basis is compromised exactly where interpretation would need grounding. And because the thundering of superhuman knights includes snakes, the target phenomenon contains an anomalous conjunction. Under damaged evidence and anomalous content, surface plausibility becomes especially unreliable. An explanation that merely sounds acceptable in familiar styles may fail precisely where deeper structure would matter most.

So the world implied by the premises is one in which novelty cannot be dismissed simply for using old words, cannot be guaranteed simply by sophisticated architecture, and is hardest to certify when evidence has been damaged and the phenomenon itself combines elements—superhuman knights, thundering, snakes—that strain ordinary explanatory habits.
